GNSS-PPP-ETL
  • Overview
  • View page source

Overview

What is GNSS-PPP-ETL?

Running Precise Point Positioning (PPP or PPP-AR) requires a constellation of auxiliary products before a single epoch can be processed: satellite orbits (SP3), clocks (CLK), phase biases (BIA), Earth rotation parameters (ERP), ionosphere maps (IONEX), troposphere grids, and antenna calibrations (ATX). These files are scattered across FTP/FTPS/HTTP servers maintained by seven IGS analysis centers, each with its own directory layout, naming convention, and update cadence.

GNSS-PPP-ETL eliminates that bookkeeping. Given a date and a processing task definition, it:

  1. Resolves every required product across all registered analysis centers

  2. Downloads and decompresses the files into a structured local workspace

  3. Tracks what was fetched and from where in reproducible lock files

  4. Optionally runs PRIDE-PPPAR end-to-end and returns parsed position output

The library is YAML-driven: products, centers, formats, and storage layouts are pure data. Adding a new analysis center or product type requires only a new YAML file — no Python changes.

Packages

This is a uv workspace with three packages:

Package

Role

gnss-management-specs

Pluggable YAML data bundle — centers, products, formats, storage layouts

gnss-product-management

Discovery, resolution, and download engine

pride-ppp

PRIDE-PPPAR integration — RINEX in, kinematic positions out

Installation

git clone https://github.com/EarthScope/GNSS-PPP-ETL.git
cd GNSS-PPP-ETL
uv sync --all-packages

Or install individual packages:

uv add gnss-product-management
uv add pride-ppp

PRIDE-PPPAR prerequisite: the pdp3 binary from PRIDE-PPPAR must be on $PATH to use pride-ppp.

Quick start

Search across all centers

from datetime import datetime, timezone
from gnss_product_management import GNSSClient

client = GNSSClient.from_defaults()
date = datetime(2025, 1, 2, tzinfo=timezone.utc)

results = (
    client.query()
    .for_product("ORBIT")
    .on(date)
    .where(TTT="FIN")                       # final solutions only
    .sources("COD", "ESA", "GFZ")          # restrict to these centers
    .prefer(TTT=["FIN", "RAP", "ULT"])      # rank by timeliness
    .search()
)

for r in results:
    print(r.center, r.quality, r.filename, r.uri)

Download products

client = GNSSClient.from_defaults(base_dir="/data/gnss-products")

paths = (
    client.query()
    .for_product("ORBIT")
    .on(date)
    .where(TTT="FIN")
    .sources("COD")
    .download(sink_id="local")
)

Resolve all dependencies for a processing task

resolution, lockfile_path = client.resolve_dependencies(
    "path/to/pride_pppar.yaml",
    date,
    sink_id="local",
)
print(resolution.summary())
print(resolution.table())

if resolution.all_required_fulfilled:
    for spec_name, path in resolution.product_paths().items():
        print(f"{spec_name}: {path}")

The resolver checks the local workspace first via lock files — subsequent calls for the same date return immediately without any network activity.

Run PRIDE-PPPAR end-to-end

from pride_ppp import PrideProcessor
from pathlib import Path

processor = PrideProcessor(
    pride_dir=Path("/data/pride"),
    output_dir=Path("/data/output"),
)
result = processor.process(Path("SITE00USA_R_20250010000_01D_30S_MO.rnx"))

if result.success:
    print(result.positions().head())   # kinematic positions as DataFrame
    print(result.residuals().head())   # residuals as DataFrame

Key concepts

Product parameters

Every GNSS product filename encodes a set of named metadata fields. The two most important query parameters are:

Parameter

Meaning

Common values

TTT

Solution timeliness

FIN (final), RAP (rapid), ULT (ultra-rapid)

AAA

Analysis center

COD, ESA, GFZ, WUM, IGS, …

Other parameters — YYYY, DDD, GPSWEEK, FMT, SMP — are computed automatically from the query date. Use .where() to pin values and .prefer() to rank without hard-filtering.

FoundResource

search() returns a list of FoundResource objects. Each represents a single candidate file — either on a remote server or already in local storage:

Property

Description

r.center

Analysis center ("COD", "ESA", …)

r.quality

Solution tier ("FIN", "RAP", …)

r.filename

Bare filename

r.uri

Full remote URL or local path

r.protocol

"ftp", "ftps", "https", or "file"

r.is_local

True if already on disk

DependencySpec

A YAML file that lists every product a processing task requires, which centers to prefer, and how to rank alternatives. Example structure:

name: pride_pppar
package: pride-ppp
task: kinematic_ppp
preferences:
  - parameter: TTT
    sorting: [FIN, RAP, ULT]
  - parameter: AAA
    sorting: [WUM, COD, ESA, GFZ]
dependencies:
  - spec: ORBIT
    required: true
  - spec: CLOCK
    required: true
  - spec: BIA
    required: false
  - spec: ERP
    required: true

Pass the path to client.resolve_dependencies() and the library handles everything else.

Lock files

Every resolved product is tracked in a sidecar <filename>_lock.json containing the source URL, SHA-256 hash, file size, and local sink path. An aggregate DependencyLockFile is written after a full dependency resolution, enabling:

  • Fast re-runs — if the lockfile already exists for a (package, task, date) triple, resolution returns immediately

  • Reproducibility — exact provenance for every file used in a processing run

  • Integrity checks — hash verification on reload

Architecture

The gnss-product-management package is organized into five layers. Each layer depends only on layers below it.

┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  Layer 4 — Interface                                    │
│  GNSSClient, ProductQuery                               │
│  ProductRegistry, WorkSpace  (environment setup)        │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│  Layer 3 — Orchestration                                │
│  SearchPlanner, WormHole, ConnectionPoolFactory         │
│  DownloadPipeline, ResolvePipeline, LockfileWriter      │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│  Layer 2 — Catalog                                      │
│  FormatCatalog, ProductCatalog, ResourceCatalog         │
│  SourcePlanner (Protocol)                               │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│  Layer 1 — Specification                                │
│  Pydantic models: FormatSpec, ProductSpec, SearchTarget │
│  DependencySpec, DependencyResolution, LockProduct      │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│  Layer 0 — Configuration                                │
│  Bundled YAML files, as_path(), hash_file(), utilities  │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

GNSSClient is the single user-facing entry point. ProductRegistry loads YAML specs and builds the catalog chain; WorkSpace registers local storage directories. Both satisfy the SourcePlanner protocol, giving SearchPlanner a uniform interface to remote and local resources.

Connection pooling (ConnectionPoolFactory) uses fsspec for FTP, FTPS, and HTTP. Cloud storage (s3://, gs://, az://) is supported transparently through cloudpathlib.

See Architecture and Class Reference for full detail.

Extensibility

Because the specification layer is pure YAML data, the library is straightforward to extend:

Add a new analysis center — write a center YAML in configs/centers/ defining its servers and product offerings, then call registry.add_resource_spec(path).

Add a new product type — add entries to product_spec.yaml and format_spec.yaml with the filename template and parameter bindings.

Add a cloud workspace — pass an S3 or GCS URI as base_dir:

client = GNSSClient.from_defaults(base_dir="s3://my-bucket/gnss-data")

All path operations — search, download, lockfile I/O — behave identically on local and cloud storage.

Swap specification sets — gnss-management-specs is an independent data package. A project with different products (e.g. acoustic ranging, seismic) can ship its own spec package and wire it into ProductRegistry without touching the engine.

Supported products

Product

Format

Description

Orbit

SP3

Precise satellite ephemerides (15 min)

Clock

CLK

Satellite & station clock corrections (30 s / 5 min)

Bias

BIA

Code and phase bias (OSB/DSB) for PPP-AR

ERP

ERP

Polar motion, UT1-UTC, LOD

GIM

IONEX

Global ionosphere TEC maps

Navigation

RINEX NAV

Broadcast ephemerides (merged BRDC)

ATX

ANTEX

Satellite & receiver antenna phase center calibrations

OBX

OBX

Satellite attitude quaternions

Troposphere

VMF1/VMF3

Vienna Mapping Functions (gridded)

Orography

GRID

Orography grids for VMF

Leap seconds

—

IERS leap second table

Sat parameters

—

Satellite metadata (mass, geometry, SRP)

Supported analysis centers

Center

Institution

Protocol

Products

CDDIS

NASA GSFC

FTPS

clock, GIM, leap seconds, navigation, orbit

COD

AIUB / Univ. Bern

FTP

bias, clock, ERP, GIM, orbit

ESA

ESA/ESOC

FTP

clock, GIM, orbit

GFZ

GFZ Potsdam

FTP

clock, orbit

IGS

IGN France

FTP / HTTPS

ATX, bias, clock, ERP, navigation, OBX, orbit

VMF

TU Wien

HTTPS

orography, VMF1, VMF3

WUM

Wuhan University

FTP

bias, clock, ERP, GIM, leap seconds, navigation, OBX, orbit, sat_parameters
